title: Using Expressions in Assignment Files
text: Using Expressions in Assignment Files Assignment files are used to configure the Uniface applications. In many cases you need several assignment files that are almost identical, except for one or two settings. To solve the problem of maintaining almost identical assignment files, you can use environment variables in assignment files. However, environment variables may not be enough as sometimes either you do not need the complete value of an environment variable, or you need to manipulate it before it can be used. To overcome such conditions, you can use expressions in Uniface assignment files. Expressions Expressions start with %%( and end with ) . This syntax is generally used inside a string in Uniface proc code to evaluate expressions inline. In the assignment file as well, you can use this syntax to evaluate expressions inline. In a proc code, you can use variables inside %%( and ) . In the assignment file, logicals declared in the [logicals] section fulfill the role of variables. An environment variable is indicated by a $ sign prefix and can be used as variables in the same way as logicals in an expression. Expressions can contain the following arithmetic operators: Operator name Operator symbol Addition + Subtraction - Multiplication * Division / Modulus % Brackets to control precedence ( … ) For string values, the following substring operators are available: Substring operator Description [x,y] Extract the characters at the positions x up to y [x:y] Extract y characters starting at position x [x] Remove characters 1 to (x-1) from the string [?x] Extract item x from a string in which tokens are separated by ? Note: The substring operators [x,y] , [x:y] , and [x] works in the in the same way as they do in proc codes. The substring operator [?x] is an additional operator specific to the assignment file expressions. The valid characters for ? are all ASCII characters that are not alphanumeric. Alphanumeric and non-ASCII characters are not valid, because then it would be the name of a logical whose value needs to be substituted. Example The following example is an assignment file snippet for the application MyApp. Assume that a user ' Someone ' is running the Uniface application MyApp on Windows and environment variable MYAPP_INSTALL points to the installation directory of MyApp . Explain this code [logicals] nr1 = 150 nr2 = 65 nr_days = %%(nr2 + 2* nr1) ; 365 message = There are %%(nr_days) in a year. ; There are 365 days in a year. home = %%($USERPROFILE) ; C:\Users\Someone root = %%(home[\1]) ; C: my_app_path = %%($MYAPP_INSTALL) ; C:\Program Files\MyApp\3.0.4 my_app_version = %%(my_app_path[\4]) ; 3.0.4 major = %%(my_app_version[.1]) ; 3 minor = %%(my_app_version[.2]) ; 0 [files] *.appext %%(home)\my_app%%(major)%%(minor)*.appext ; *.appext C:\Users\Someone\myapp30*.appext [resources] %%(my_app_path)\usys\myapp.uar Copy Scope When reading and interpreting an assignment file, Uniface first processes [logicals] section. This means that logicals that are defined in it can be used in all other sections, even if they precede the [logicals] section in which they are defined. However, inside the [logicals] section the order is important. You can only use logicals as variables in an expression in the [logicals] section that have already been declared in it previously. If you are including another assignment file using the #file directive, and need to use logicals from that file in an expression in the [logicals] section of the including assignment file, then the #file directive must precede the [logicals] section in which you use those logicals. Besides these rules, logicals defined in usys.asn file can be used in expressions in other application assignment files, but not the other way around. Table 1.
